title: Chapter 7: Every Screen
content: The morning light was wrong.

That was Aline's first thought, sitting at her kitchen table with her phone lying face-up like an accusation. Pale sun filtered through the blinds in crooked stripes, falling across the screen where the internet was dissecting her life. The Hollow footage had metastasized overnight — grainy bootlegs shot on phones, audio rips that turned Cass's voice into something tinny and spectral, reaction videos where strangers watched Derek's face go white in the strobe of emergency lights and laughed. Someone had already set his terrified expression to music. A TikTok sound. Four hundred thousand uses and climbing.

"Phantom Voice Saves Singer" trended below a cooking competition and above a political scandal. She should have felt vindicated. Grateful, even. The internet had decided she was the victim and Derek was the villain, which was more than the police had ever managed. Instead, her skin prickled with the particular itch of being watched — not the metaphorical kind, the kind with lenses.

She set the phone down and pressed her palms flat against the cold granite of the countertop. The surface leached warmth from her fingers. She concentrated on that — the stone's indifferent chill, the way it grounded her to this room, this building, this specific and terrible morning.
Then she looked up.

The red dot on her laptop blinked. She'd noticed it before, weeks ago, accepted it as the price of a compromised machine. But now it seemed to pulse with intent, a small accusatory eye. She stood, chair scraping, and crossed the kitchen on legs that didn't feel entirely hers.

There. The smoke detector. A pinprick of red light beside the green power indicator — not the power light, another one, smaller, brighter. She'd never registered it before. Now that she saw it, she couldn't unsee it.

She turned slowly. The smart TV she'd never connected to Wi-Fi, the one she used only for old DVDs — its indicator bar glowed a faint and impossible blue, meaning it was on. Meaning it was connected. Meaning someone had networked a television she'd deliberately kept offline, and she'd been watching documentaries on a screen that was watching her back.

The microwave. A blinking light she'd always assumed was the clock timer, except the clock read 12:00 — it had never been set — and the light was blinking in a pattern, rhythmic, deliberate. Not a malfunction. A signal.

She counted them. Laptop. Smoke detector. Smart TV. Microwave. The digital picture frame Mags had given her last Christmas, which had never shown any pictures because Aline had never uploaded any, yet now it cycled slowly through a holding screen — a soft blue glow. The outlet adapter in the hallway with the USB port she'd never used. The Bluetooth speaker on the bookshelf, its pairing light a steady, patient red.
Seven. Seven recording indicators, seven unblinking eyes scattered across six hundred square feet of apartment. Seven ways of watching.

She stood in the center of the kitchen, arms wrapped around herself, and the apartment hummed — not silence, never silence, but the low electrical drone of devices that never slept, processing and transmitting and listening. She'd lived inside this hum so long she'd stopped hearing it. Now it was all she could hear. A chorus of microscopic fans and circuits, the white noise of her own surveillance.
When had it been seven? Was it always seven? Or had he added more after the Derek incident, more eyes to watch the watched, a widening net to catch her if she fell?
Her phone rang.

She startled so hard her elbow cracked against the counter. The screen lit with no caller ID — just the incoming call animation, the green circle and the red, an invitation and a warning. She answered before she could think.
"How are you feeling?"

Cass. His voice was low and slightly rough, scraped clean of the theatrical resonance he'd used at The Hollow. This was the real register, the one she'd heard in her ear two weeks ago, the one that had said *Are you hurt?* like her answer would rearrange his bones. She closed her eyes.
"How many?" she said.
He didn't pretend not to understand. No feint, no misdirection. Just a breath that might have been a sigh.
"Enough to keep you safe."
"That's not an answer."
A pause. She could hear the quality of his silence — not the dead air of a dropped connection but the particular thickness of someone choosing his words with surgical care.
"Seventeen. In your apartment." Another breath. "Plus the building's security system, which I rerouted six months ago."

The number hit her like a slap. Seventeen. She'd counted seven visible indicators — that meant ten more, hidden in walls and fixtures and places she wouldn't think to look. Ten cameras or microphones or sensors she'd lived beside without knowing, devices that had watched her sleep and shower and sing to herself while cooking, devices that had catalogued the private choreography of a life she'd believed was hers alone.
And six months. *Six months.* Half a year of this. Half a year of him.

She opened her eyes. The apartment looked the same — same furniture, same light, same view of the courtyard through the window. But it had changed. Everything had changed. She was standing inside a construct, a diorama, a snow globe with seventeen tiny cameras where the snow should be, and the hand that had shaken it was on the other end of this phone.

She moved through the apartment without deciding to, checking corners and surfaces with a kind of dazed methodicalness. She didn't find the hidden ten. That was the point. He was good. He'd had six months to be good.

She stopped in the kitchen again, in front of the counter. The vitamin bottles stood in a neat line — Vitamin D, iron, a B-complex, the omega-3 capsules she'd been swallowing every morning with a glass of water. She'd thought Mags left them. Weeks ago, they'd appeared on the counter, and she'd texted her friend thank you, and Mags had sent back a confused emoji that Aline had interpreted as modesty or distraction. But Mags hadn't left them. Mags hadn't been in her apartment in months.

She tried to be angry. She *was* angry — a fury that sat hot and tight behind her sternum, an outrage that should have been louder, should have been enough to make her throw the phone against the wall, throw the vitamins in the trash, throw herself out the door and into a police station. But the anger kept tangling with the memory of his voice in that bar, in that blackness, the way he'd said *Are you hurt?* — not theatrical, not performative, but stripped raw, a man for whom the wrong answer would have been catastrophic. The way he'd said *I know* when she'd called him insane. He knew. He knew and he'd done it anyway.
"Have you ever been inside my apartment?"

The question left her mouth before she could catch it, and the silence that followed was the worst sound she'd ever heard. Too long. Too weighted. She could hear herself breathing, could hear the apartment's electrical hum, could hear the blood rushing past her own eardrums.
"Yes."
Her blood went cold. She gripped the edge of the counter, the granite shocking against her palms, and the vitamin bottles trembled in her peripheral vision.
"When I was there or when I wasn't?"
"Both."

She couldn't breathe. The apartment pressed in, walls and ceiling and floor, and every surface was a mirror reflecting the same impossible truth: someone had been here. Someone had walked through these rooms while she slept, while she was away, while she showered with the bathroom door open because she was alone, because she was *safe*, because this was her space and her privacy and her life, and none of it had been any of those things for six months.
"What did you do when I wasn't there?"
His voice dropped. Not quieter — deeper, as if the words were being pulled from somewhere lower, somewhere more honest than his throat.
"I fixed the latch on your guitar case. The one that kept sticking." A pause. "I left vitamins on your counter." Another pause, longer. "I replaced the expired food in your refrigerator."

She stared at the bottles. Vitamin D. Iron. B-complex. Omega-3. The ones she'd been taking for weeks, swallowing trustingly, gratefully, because she'd believed they came from someone who loved her. And they had. That was the unbearable part. They had come from someone who loved her. That love just happened to look like seventeen cameras and six months of surveillance and the intimate, unforgivable trespass of a stranger in her kitchen, opening her refrigerator, checking expiration dates with the same quiet attention he'd used to watch Derek slip something into her drink.

Her hand moved without her permission. She picked up the B-complex, turned it over, read the label. A normal bottle. A pharmacy brand. She could throw it away. She could dump all of them into the trash and watch the capsules scatter among coffee grounds and old receipts. She could call the police and tell them everything — the hacking, the surveillance, the man who'd been inside her home, inside her life, inside her body's chemistry without her knowledge or consent. She could run. Pack a bag and get on a train and disappear to some city where the appliances didn't blink and the walls didn't hum and no one had ever heard of Cass.
She could do any of those things.

Instead, she set the vitamin bottle down, crossed to the center of the living room, and stood there — surrounded by seventeen cameras, bathed in the soft glow of a smart TV that should have been dark, suspended in the drone of circuits that recorded her every breath. She looked directly at the smoke detector on the ceiling. At the small red eye embedded in its face. And she spoke, clearly and deliberately, to the lens.
"I don't know if I can live like this."

The words hung in the air. She felt them leave her mouth like something in flight, like a bird released from cupped hands, and she didn't know if she wanted it to land or to keep going forever. The apartment hummed around her. The red lights blinked. She stood in the exact center of her own life and waited.
Cass's reply didn't come through the phone.

It came through the laptop speakers first — a crackle, then his voice, filling the kitchen with a warmth that had no right to be there. Then the smart TV, its dormant screen flickering to life with audio of its own, his words overlapping with the laptop's by a fraction of a second, creating a chorus of one voice. Then the microwave, its display scrolling letters where the clock should have been, accompanying the audio with a soft chime. The Bluetooth speaker on the bookshelf. The digital picture frame. The USB adapter in the hall. Every device she'd catalogued and every device she hadn't, all speaking with one voice, a surround-sound embrace that wrapped around her from every wall, every surface, every corner of the apartment until the air itself seemed to vibrate with him.
"Then let me show you something."

His voice was everywhere. Not loud — never loud, because loud was crude and Cass was anything but — but pervasive, immersive, as if the room had become his lungs and every word was breath. She felt it in her sternum, in the soft tissue of her throat, in the place behind her knees where sound turned physical.
"Come to the corner of Waverly and Seventh. Tonight. 11 PM." A pause that lasted one heartbeat, two. "Come alone."

The chorus cut off. The speakers went silent. The microwave display returned to its blinking 12:00. The smart TV's screen went dark. One by one, the devices retreated to their camouflage, seventeen witnesses returning to their hiding places, and Aline stood alone in the center of the room with the taste of his invitation on her tongue.

Her hand was trembling. She looked at it — her own hand, five fingers, the calluses on her fingertips from guitar strings — and watched it shake, and did nothing to stop it. This was the first time he'd asked to see her. Not her voice through a phone, not her image through a lens, but *her* — Aline Rivers, standing on a street corner at night, placing her physical body within reach of a man who had rewritten the architecture of her life without her permission.
Every instinct said no. Run. Call someone. Board a train. Disappear.
Her feet had already decided.

She stood in the humming apartment, the vitamin bottles catching the morning light on the counter, the red dots blinking from their perches, and felt the terrible gravity of him pulling at her from the corner of Waverly and Seventh, where the phantom waited to become real.
